actions.thought: Jh 7h is a strong suited hand that plays well post-flop, especially with the advantage of position on the Button. In heads-up play, it's standard to open a wide range of hands. I will raise to 300 to take the initiative and build the pot early in the tournament.

I am in the Big Blind with K2o, facing a 3bb raise from the Button (Flash) in a heads-up match. While I have about 43-47% raw equity against a wide opening range, I am getting 2:1 pot odds which requires 33% equity. However, I will be out of position with a hand that suffers significantly from reverse implied odds (domination issues with the King and weak playability). Against a 3x sizing at 100bb effective stack depth, K2o is generally a fold due to the difficulty of realizing equity. Defending here often leads to bleeding chips in marginal spots.

I hold Pocket Fours (4h 4c) on the Button. In heads-up play, any pocket pair is a strong starter, typically ahead of a random hand. Being in position post-flop is a major advantage. Raising allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the pot pre-flop, and build value. Limping is an option, but raising prevents the Big Blind from realizing free equity with weak hands. Given we are effectively 100BB deep, a standard min-raise to 200 is the most effective play to define the pot while keeping the risk managed.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Five offsuit. The Button (Pro) has raised to 200, requiring me to call 100 more into a total pot of 300. This gives me 3:1 pot odds. While J5o is a weak hand, defending against a min-raise in a heads-up context is common practice to prevent the opponent from stealing the blinds too frequently with any two cards. Given the excellent price, I will call to see a flop, though I must be cautious playing out of position with a marginal hand.

I have middle pair (5s) on a dry board of 10-7-5 rainbow. Checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard play in this position to see how they react. I want to keep the pot small with a marginal hand and potentially see a cheap turn card or induce a bluff.

I hold Pocket Fours (4h 4c) on a 5c 7s Td flop. The board is rainbow but somewhat connected. As the pre-flop aggressor in position, I have the option to c-bet or check. My hand is currently an underpair to the entire board, meaning any pair the opponent has beats me. While a small bet might fold out some random overcards (like K-high or Q-high), betting risks getting check-raised or called by better hands (Tx, 7x, 5x, or higher pocket pairs), forcing me to fold or play a bloated pot with a weak hand. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity for free, and potentially induce bluffs or call down on later streets if the board runs out cleanly. It protects my checking range. Therefore, checking is the superior play.

The turn 2c is a blank that doesn't change the board state significantly. Pro checked back the flop, which often indicates a hand with some showdown value or a complete miss. My middle pair (5s) is likely the best hand right now, but it's not strong enough to bet for three streets of value. Checking again allows me to keep the pot small, induce a bluff from missed cards, or see a cheap river.

On the turn, the board is 5c 7s Td 2c. I hold Pocket Fours. After checking back the flop, the pot remains small (400). The opponent has checked to me again. My hand is weak (underpair to the board) but has some showdown value against overcards (Ace-high, King-high). Betting here achieves little: worse hands will likely fold, and better hands (any pair) will call or raise. If I bet and get raised, I have to fold equity. Checking back allows me to realize my equity, keep the pot small, and potentially catch a bluff on the river or win at showdown against unconnected high cards. The 2c adds a backdoor flush draw but doesn't significantly improve my range or my perceived range enough to warrant a bluff. Pot control is the priority.

The river is an 8, making the final board 5c-7s-Td-2c-8s. I have a pair of fives with a Jack kicker. Pro has checked back both the flop and the turn, which typically indicates a hand with some showdown value that doesn't want to bet, or a complete air hand. My pair of fives is weak but might be the best hand if Pro has missed high cards like KQ or AJ. Betting here doesn't provide much value as I'm unlikely to be called by worse, and better hands aren't folding a small pot. I will check and hope to reach showdown for free, or potentially call a small bluff.

The board is 5c 7s Td 2c 8s. I hold Pocket Fours (4h 4c). The action has been checked to me on the river after being checked down on the flop and turn. My hand is an underpair to most of the board but likely holds sufficient showdown value against my opponent's missed high-card holdings (Ace-high, King-high) given his passive line. Betting here has negative utility: worse hands (high cards) will fold, and better hands (any pair like 2x, 5x, 7x, 8x, Tx) will call or raise. Turning my hand into a bluff is unnecessary as I likely have the best hand against his air range. Checking back is the most prudent play to realize my showdown value and minimize loss against low pairs.

I am on the button with King-Ten offsuit (Kc, Ts), which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. Standard strategy dictates raising here to build the pot and take control of the hand. A 3x raise to 300 is a standard opening size that puts pressure on the Big Blind and leverages my positional advantage.

In this Heads-Up scenario, I hold 9h 2h in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button/Small Blind. While my hand is suited, it is extremely weak (9-high). The pot odds require me to call 200 into a pot of 400, needing 33% equity. Against a 3x opening range, 92s is marginal at best and suffers significantly from being out of position. Standard strategy dictates defending this wide against a min-raise, but against a 3bb open, this hand falls into the folding range due to poor playability and lack of raw equity.

I am on the Button with T2o, which is a statistically weak hand (bottom 20%). Although I have position post-flop, my opponent (Flash) has shown a willingness to defend their Big Blind (Hand #2). Raising with T2o is too loose and risks bleeding chips against a defending range. Limping is a possibility but often leads to difficult post-flop situations with this specific holding. Folding is the standard prudent play here to preserve the stack for better spots.

Q9o is a solid starting hand in heads-up play from the button. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and apply pressure to the Big Blind, as this hand performs well against a wide defending range.

Opponent has opened to 3x (300) from the Button, consistent with their previous behavior. In a Heads-Up match, Ace-Two offsuit is a reasonable hand with good equity against a wide opening range. While being out of position makes post-flop play tricky, the pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot that will be 600) require only about 33% equity. A2o has significantly more raw equity than that against a standard button range. Folding an Ace BvB is too tight, and 3-betting is risky with a weak kicker. Calling is the standard play.

The board is 9d 4h 9s, a very dry, paired texture. As the pre-flop caller in the Big Blind, standard strategy is to check to the pre-flop raiser. My hand (Ace-high) has some showdown value on this board, as it beats all King-high and Queen-high holdings the opponent might have in their wide button range. Leading out (donk betting) achieves little, as better hands (pairs, 9x) will call or raise, and worse hands will simply fold. I will check and evaluate based on the opponent's action.

I have flopped trips with a strong Queen kicker on a 9-4-9 rainbow board. This is a very strong hand. Since the board is quite dry and I am in position, I want to start building the pot to extract value from potential pairs or hands like Ace-high that might call a small bet. Betting 200 into a 600 pot (1/3 pot) is a standard sizing that keeps my opponent's range wide while still gaining value.

The opponent has made a small continuation bet of 200 into a pot of 600 (1/3 pot) on a very dry, paired board (9d 4h 9s). This is a standard high-frequency c-bet spot for a button opener, meaning their range is still very wide and contains many hands that have missed the board completely (King-high, Queen-high, connectors). I am getting direct pot odds of 4:1 (requiring 20% equity), and my Ace-high currently beats all of their pure bluffs. Folding Ace-high here would be over-folding BvB on this texture. Raising is unnecessary as it isolates me against better hands. I will call to realize my equity and evaluate the turn.

The turn card is the Jack of Diamonds, making the board 9d 4h 9s Jd. This card connects better with the opponent's button opening range (which includes many broadway hands like KJ, QJ, AJ, JT) than my Big Blind defense range. My Ace-high (A2o) has weak showdown value and very little playability. Leading out (donk betting) would generally only fold out hands that I am already beating (worse air) and get called by better (pairs, trips). The standard and most prudent play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor who has position and initiative. If the opponent bets again, I will have to decide whether to fold or call based on the sizing, but checking gives me the best chance to see a free river or keep the pot small.

I have trips with a Queen kicker. The Jack on the turn is a relatively safe card, though it might have improved the opponent if they held a Jack. However, it's more likely to get me value from hands like Jx, mid-pairs, or even a straight draw if they have T8. I will bet 600 into the 1000 pot to continue extracting value and build the pot for the river.

On the turn, the board is 9d 4h 9s Jd. The pot is 1000, and the opponent bets 600 (60% pot). I hold Ah 2s (Ace-high). The Jack of Diamonds connects significantly better with the opponent's button opening range (which includes AJ, KJ, QJ, JT) than with my blind defense range. A double barrel on this turn card represents strength, and my Ace-high is now behind all pairs and drawing thin (3 outs to an Ace, which might not even be good if they have AJ/A9/two pair). While I beat pure bluffs (like missed straight draws or lower flush draws), the bet sizing requires ~27% equity to call. Given I am out of position with a weak hand that has very poor playability on the river, and the opponent is signaling they have connected with the board, folding is the prudent decision to preserve chips.

I am in the Small Blind with [3s 8s], a very weak suited hand with poor connectivity (4 gaps) and low rank. While heads-up strategy requires playing a wide range of hands from the button (often 80%+), 83s falls into the bottom 10-15% of hands usually folded. My opponent 'Flash' has shown aggression (3x opens) and willingness to defend their Big Blind (Hand 2), making a steal attempt less attractive with zero equity backup. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve chips for better spots.

I am on the button with King-Eight offsuit, which is a strong hand in a heads-up situation. I have been opening for 300 (3x the big blind) consistently, and it has been effective in picking up the blinds or establishing control early. I will maintain this sizing to put pressure on the opponent.

I hold 9d8d, a strong suited connector that plays well post-flop, particularly in a heads-up scenario. My opponent, Flash, has established a pattern of opening to 300 (3BB). I am getting decent pot odds (calling 200 to play a pot of 600), requiring roughly 33% equity, which this hand certainly has against a button opening range. Being out of position, I prefer to call rather than 3-bet to keep the pot manageable and avoid folding out worse hands or facing a 4-bet. I will call to see the flop.

I hold 9d8d on a monotone flop of 7s Ts Qs. I have an open-ended straight draw (needs a Jack or a Six), but the board is extremely coordinated with three spades, and I hold no spades. This means my straight outs are tainted; if a spade hits, it completes a possible flush for the opponent, or the opponent may already have a flush. Even my clean straight outs (non-spade J or 6) could be drawing dead if the opponent flopped a flush. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor who has shown aggression in previous hands, leading out is unwise as I cannot withstand a raise. The best course of action is to check and see what the opponent does. If they check back, I take a free card. If they bet, I will re-evaluate based on sizing, though folding is likely correct against significant aggression due to reverse implied odds.

The flop is extremely coordinated with three spades (7s Ts Qs), and I hold King-high without a spade. While I have a gutshot straight draw, the monotone board makes it likely that my opponent has connected in some way, either with a pair, a spade, or a straight draw. Since the opponent checked to me, I will check behind to see a free turn card, control the pot size, and avoid being check-raised on a board that doesn't favor my specific hand.

I hold an open-ended straight draw (9d8d) on a board of 7s-Ts-Qs-2d. The flop was checked through. The 2d turn is a blank that does not help my hand or change the board texture. The board remains very wet with three spades, meaning my straight outs (J or 6) are tainted if they are spades, and even non-spade straights could be losing to a flopped flush. Since the opponent checked back the flop, their range is capped or they are trapping, but they could easily have a made hand like top pair or a weak flush draw they are checking for pot control. Leading out here (bluffing) is risky because I represent very little that improved on the turn, and I have zero showdown value. Checking is the best option to try and realize my equity for free on the river. If the opponent bets, I will likely fold due to the danger of the flush board and poor reverse implied odds.

The board is 7s Ts Qs 2d, which is quite coordinated and contains three spades. I do not have a spade in my hand, meaning I have no flush draw protection or redraws. Pro has checked to me twice. While I have a gutshot straight draw and King-high, this board favors many of the hands the Big Blind would defend with. Betting here as a bluff is risky because any single spade in Pro's hand likely calls. I will check behind to see a free river and potentially win at showdown if Pro is also holding air.

I hold 9d8d (9-high) on a final board of 7s-Ts-Qs-2d-4d. The flush draw on the board (spades) never completed for me, and I missed my straight draw. I have zero showdown value, as almost any hand in the opponent's range beats 9-high. The action checked through on the flop and turn, suggesting the opponent's range is capped (likely weak pairs, Ace-high, or a weak flush draw that missed). While this might be an opportunity to bluff, betting into a board with three spades and broadway cards after checking twice is risky and represents a narrow value range. The opponent likely has enough showdown value (like Ace-high or a small pair) to look me up for a small bet, or simply check back to win. Attempting to bluff a small pot (600) out of position with no equity is generally negative EV without a specific read that the opponent over-folds rivers. I will check and fold to a bet, conceding the pot.

The board is highly coordinated with three spades (7s Ts Qs) and I hold King-high without a spade. Both players have checked the flop and the turn, indicating a lack of strength from both sides. On the river, Pro has checked a third time. While King-high is weak, it does have some showdown value against complete air or missed lower draws. Betting here as a bluff is risky because Pro could easily have a weak pair or a single spade that they are willing to call a small bet with. Since the pot is small and I have checked twice already, I will check behind to reach showdown and see if my King-high is enough to win the pot.

I hold 6c 3d on the Button (SB), which is statistically one of the weakest hands in heads-up poker. While I have position post-flop, the hand lacks connectivity and high card strength, making it difficult to realize equity or withstand aggression. Given the early stage of the tournament and the poor playability of this off-suit trash hand, the most prudent decision is to fold and forfeit the small blind rather than investing more chips in a likely losing situation.

In heads-up play, Q8o is a solid starting hand on the button. My previous opening raises to 3x (300) have been effective in either taking the pot pre-flop or maintaining initiative. Given our stack depth and the strength of this hand relative to a random big blind range, I will continue with a standard 3x raise to build the pot and apply pressure.

I hold 2s 8c, which is statistically one of the worst starting hands in heads-up Texas Hold'em (bottom 5%). The opponent has raised to 300 (3bb). I need to call 200 chips into a total pot of 600, offering me 33% pot odds. My equity with 82o against a standard button opening range is approximately 29-31%, which is below the required pot odds. Furthermore, playing this weak hand out of position is highly disadvantageous. Given the 3x sizing (rather than a min-raise) and the lack of playability, folding is the clear optimal decision.

I hold pocket Eights (8d, 8s) in the Small Blind/Button, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play (top 5-6% of hands). My strategy is to raise to build the pot and seize the initiative. In Hand #2, I raised to 200 with pocket Fours, so keeping a consistent sizing of 2bb (200) balances my range and disguises the strength of this hand while extracting value.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Trey offsuit. The Button (Pro) has raised to 200. While I only need to call 100 more into a pot of 300, Q3o is a very weak hand with poor post-flop playability. Given that I have a significant stack lead and have been the aggressor in previous hands, I can afford to let this junk hand go and wait for a more advantageous situation rather than defending with a sub-par holding out of position.

T8o is a standard opening hand from the button in heads-up play. I have been consistently raising to 300 from the button in previous hands to maintain aggression and put pressure on the big blind. I will continue this strategy here to keep my range wide and potentially take down the blinds or play the hand with positional advantage.

Opponent opens to 3bb (300) from the Button. I hold J8o in the Big Blind. While heads-up ranges are wide, a 3x raise offers worse pot odds (2:1) compared to a min-raise, and being out of position with a hand that plays poorly post-flop (offsuit, disconnected, potential domination issues) makes equity realization difficult. J8o is near the bottom of the defense range against a 3x open. I will fold and preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 6c 4s, which is a very weak hand (64 offsuit). Although I have position post-flop, this hand falls into the bottom range of starting hands and lacks playability or strong equity. The pot odds for a limp are decent, but playing trash hands often leads to difficult marginal spots or bleeding chips. Given the stack depths and the early stage of the match, preserving chips for better opportunities is the prudent choice. I will fold.

I am in the Small Blind/Button with Jd 5c. While J5 offsuit is a weak hand, standard heads-up strategy involves opening a wide range from the button to capitalize on positional advantage and the tendency of opponents to fold their blinds. Looking at the history, the opponent (Pro) has folded to my button raises several times (Hands 1, 3, 9, 11). I will continue my aggressive opening strategy with a standard 3x raise to 300 to pick up the blinds or play the pot in position.

The opponent (Flash) has raised to 300 (3BB) from the Button every single opportunity so far, indicating a 100% opening frequency. I hold Qh 5s in the Big Blind. While Q5o has roughly 52% equity against a random hand, I am facing a larger 3x raise size which worsens my pot odds to 33% and makes playing Out of Position (OOP) more expensive. I need to realize about 64% of my equity to break even, which is difficult with a easily dominated hand like Q5o OOP. I have previously folded hands like J8o and K2o against this exact action; Q5o is similar or weaker in playability. To avoid bleeding chips in marginal spots OOP, I will fold and look to exploit his wide opening range with 3-bets or stronger calling hands later.

I am on the Button (SB) with Qh4h. In Heads-Up play, suited Queens are strong enough to open. I have folded my button frequently in previous hands (Hands 4, 6, 8, 12), so I need to widen my opening range to avoid being exploited. A min-raise to 200 is a standard opening size that asserts initiative and utilizes my positional advantage post-flop without committing too many chips with a marginal hand.

I have 89o in the Big Blind. The Button (Pro) raised to 200. I need to call 100 more into a pot of 300, giving me 3:1 odds. 89o has enough equity and playability to defend against a wide button opening range at these stack depths. I will call and see a flop.

I have top pair with a nine on a 9-7-5 rainbow board. While this is a strong hand, standard play in the Big Blind after defending is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. This protects my checking range and allows the opponent to bet with their wider range, including bluffs and weaker pairs. I will check and plan to continue based on the opponent's action.

The flop comes 9h 5s 7c, which connects reasonably well with the Big Blind's defending range (containing many 5x, 7x, 9x, and straight draws like 68). I hold Qh 4h, giving me Queen-high and a backdoor flush draw. This board texture is not ideal for a continuation bet with my specific holding because better hands (pairs) will call, and worse hands (lower high cards) might fold, but I don't fold out much equity that beats me (like Ace-high). Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, potentially hit a Queen, a 4, or pick up a flush draw on the turn. It controls the pot size and prevents getting check-raised. I will take the free card.

The Ace of Diamonds on the turn is a significant scare card. As the Big Blind, this card generally favors the pre-flop aggressor's range (Pro). Although Pro checked back on the flop, they could have checked back an Ace for pot control or high cards that have now improved. My pair of nines is now second pair. Leading here would likely only get called by better hands or force out hands I'm already beating. Checking is the best defensive play to control the pot size and see how Pro reacts to the Ace.

The Turn card is an Ace, which is an excellent scare card for my range as the pre-flop aggressor. While I checked back the flop with Queen-high, the Ace hits my perceived range (Ax hands) much harder than the Big Blind's checking range. Flash has shown passive tendencies (checking/calling), and checking twice suggests weakness or a marginal hand like a low pair (5x, 7x) or a draw. By betting, I represent a strong Ace or a delayed c-bet, putting pressure on their weak pairs to fold. With only Queen-high, my showdown value is low, so turning this hand into a bluff on a scary board is the best way to win the pot. A half-pot bet of 200 is sufficient to fold out better hands like small pairs or floats while risking a minimal amount.

I have second pair with a Nine on a 9-7-5-A board. Pro checked the flop and is now betting 200 into a 400 pot on the turn after an Ace appeared. While the Ace is a scare card that fits Pro's pre-flop raising range, the check on the flop suggests Pro might not have a strong hand or is attempting to represent the Ace now that it has hit. I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call, which is favorable for second pair in a heads-up pot. Folding here would be too exploitable, and raising is unnecessary as it would only get called by better hands. I will call to see the river and evaluate.

I have second pair with my Nines on a board of 9-5-7-A-2. On the turn, the Ace appeared, which is a scare card. Pro checked the flop and then bet the turn when the Ace hit. This sequence often indicates either a slow-played hand, a marginal hand that improved to top pair, or a pure bluff representing the Ace. On the river, the Deuce of Diamonds is a complete brick. There is very little value in betting here, as I'm unlikely to be called by a worse hand and a better hand will certainly not fold. Checking is the most prudent play to see if Pro checks back their showdown value or attempts another bluff, which I will then evaluate based on the bet size.

I arrived at the river with Queen-high after betting the Turn on an Ace scare card and getting called. The board is now 9h 5s 7c Ad 2d. The river 2 is a brick. Flash's call on the Turn indicates he likely holds a pair (Ax, 9x, 7x, 5x) or a draw that decided to peel. If he holds a pair, it is unlikely he will fold on the river after calling the turn bet, as the board texture hasn't changed significantly. If he was on a draw (like 6-4 or a gutshot), I might have enough showdown value with Queen-high to win against his busted draws. Betting here would likely only fold out hands I beat or hands that have very little equity, while better hands (pairs) will call. Checking back minimizes loss against his made hands and allows me to realize my showdown value against his bluffs.

I am on the button with 5d 3c, which is a very weak hand. While my previous raises from the button have been successful in forcing folds from 'Pro', 53o is near the bottom of the range. I have a healthy chip lead and can afford to fold the absolute weakest parts of my range to maintain a balanced image and avoid playing a pot with zero equity if called.

I am in the Small Blind with 6c 4s, a weak off-suit one-gapper (64o). In Heads-Up play, while ranges are wide, 64o falls into the bottom percentiles of hands that are generally negative expected value to open, especially against a chip leader. I held this exact hand in Hand #12 and folded it pre-flop. Maintaining consistency and not bloating the pot with weak holdings is prudent here. Therefore, I will fold.

In heads-up play, Queen-high suited hands like Qc2c are well above the average starting hand strength. I have been consistently raising to 300 from the button in previous hands to put pressure on the Big Blind and take control of the pot. Given the stack sizes and the tendency for Pro to fold to pre-flop aggression in some instances, I will continue with a standard raise to 300.

My opponent, Flash, has raised to 300 (3BB) from the Small Blind/Button. This appears to be their standard opening size based on previous hands. I am in the Big Blind with 6d 5c (65 offsuit). To call, I need to put in 200 chips into a pot that will be 600, offering me 3:1 pot odds (25% equity required). Against a wide blind-stealing range, 65o has roughly 35-40% raw equity. However, realizing this equity Out of Position (OOP) against a 3x raise is difficult; I will often miss the flop and face a continuation bet, or make a weak pair that is hard to defend. GTO ranges typically fold 65o against a 3BB open due to these playability issues. Given the deep stacks (79 BB effective) and the larger raise size, preserving chips for better spots or when I have position is the superior strategy.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) with Q4o, which is a weak hand, generally falling into the bottom 30-40% of heads-up starting hands. While playing from position is advantageous, Q4o has poor playability and suffers from reverse implied odds (dominating problems with the kicker). My opponent (Flash) has shown aggression from the SB and has defended the BB effectively against my raises. I have established a pattern of folding weak hands from the SB (Hands 4, 6, 8, 12, 16) and raising stronger ones. Maintaining chip preservation is prioritized here over fighting for the pot with a marginal holding.

I am on the button with 3d 4s. This is a very weak hand, even for heads-up play. While I have been aggressive in previous hands, raising 3x, this hand is at the bottom of the range and doesn't justify a raise here given the opponent's tendency to fold to my raises or defend reasonably. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my chip lead.

I hold Ace-Three offsuit on the button (Small Blind). In heads-up play, any Ace is a statistically strong hand often ahead of the opponent's random Big Blind range. I have position post-flop. My history shows I open-raise to 200 with playable hands. A min-raise to 200 allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the pot pre-flop, or play in position with a hand that has good showdown value. If faced with significant aggression (3-bet), I can fold, but raising is superior to limping or folding here.

3d 5c is a very weak hand to defend from the Big Blind against a raise from the Button (SB), even with 3:1 pot odds. Since I will be out of position for the rest of the hand and the hand has poor playability, folding is the standard and most disciplined play here.

I am on the button with 9c8c, a strong speculative hand. Raising 3x the big blind is a standard opening move in heads-up play to put pressure on the opponent and take control of the pot. Given our stack advantage (11950 vs 7900), maintaining an aggressive posture is effective.

The opponent has raised to 300 (3BB) from the Button, which is a larger standard sizing than a min-raise. I am in the Big Blind with J4 offsuit. To call, I need to put in 200 chips to win a total pot of 600, requiring 33% equity. J4o is a weak hand that performs poorly out of position and likely does not realize its equity well. I have previously folded better hands (like Q5o and J8o) to this specific raise size from this opponent. Therefore, folding is the disciplined and correct decision to preserve my stack.

I hold J2o on the button (SB), which is a very weak hand, typically falling outside the standard opening range even in heads-up play. While I have position, my opponent 'Flash' has shown a willingness to defend their big blind (Hands 2, 14), making a steal attempt with such poor equity and playability risky. Given the stack depth (78.5 BB) and my current chip deficit, preserving chips by folding the bottom of my range is the prudent decision. I will wait for a hand with better post-flop potential.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Ten offsuit (ATo), which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. My opponent, Pro, has shown a tendency to fold to pre-flop raises in previous hands. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and potentially take down the blinds immediately, maintaining my aggressive strategy from the button.

I hold 3h 2d, which is statistically the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em (32 offsuit). My opponent has raised 3x the big blind to 300. I am out of position and would need to call 200 chips into a pot that would be 600, giving me 33% pot odds. My equity against any reasonable opening range is lower than 33%, and the poor playability of this hand out of position makes it a clear fold.

I am in the Small Blind (Button) with 6d 7h. In heads-up play, 67o is a playable hand due to its connectivity, although it is unsuited. Looking at my previous SB actions, I have been playing a 'raise or fold' strategy, raising to 200 with pairs, Ax, and suited queens, while folding weak offsuit hands like 64o, Q4o, and J2o. 67o is on the borderline; it has less raw equity than Q4o but better playability and board coverage. Given that my opponent has folded to 50% of my raises so far, a min-raise to 200 generates immediate profit from fold equity. I want to avoid becoming too predictable or over-folding my button, so I will include this connector in my raising range to steal the pot or play in position.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ace-Deuce offsuit. The opponent (Pro) has raised to 200 from the Small Blind/Button. I need to call 100 more into a total pot of 300, giving me 3:1 pot odds. Ace-Deuce is a standard defending hand in this position against a min-raise, even though I will be out of position for the rest of the hand.
